summary: My mother, Catherine Jones, was a beautiful and well-educated woman. She worked as an elevator operator for the Tulsa World when she was about 18 or 20 years old. We lived next door to her mother, my grandmother, in Tulsa. She was a strong presence in our family of five boys and one girl.

I was born and raised in Tulsa, Oklahoma, right in the house next to my grandmother at 4912 West 8th Street. My family lived there during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921, and my father was called in with the National Guard to help keep order. I attended school in Tulsa, including James Whitcomb Riley School on Charles Page Boulevard and later Central High School at 6th and Cincinnati.

My father, Earl Ray Calvert, was in the National Guard and was called in during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921. He was stationed guarding the railroad tracks to keep them open and told me stories about a stray bullet hitting his buddy's head. He never spoke about the violence or burning during the riot, only his duty to protect the railroad.

My father, Earl Ray Calvert, was about 20 years older than me and was an expert machinist who owned a machine shop at 1st and Lansing in Tulsa. He served in the National Guard during the Tulsa race massacre in 1921, guarding railroad tracks. During World War II, he taught young machinists how to make high-pressure oil field pumps, working long hours to support the war effort.

The Tulsa race massacre happened on May 31 and June 1, 1921, when my father was called in with the National Guard. He was assigned to guard the railroad tracks and kept them open during the chaos. Although he never spoke about the shootings or burning, he shared a story about a stray bullet hitting his buddy’s head during that time.

My father owned and ran a machine shop located at 1st and Lansing in Tulsa. He was a skilled machinist who specialized in making oil field pumps that could handle thousands of pounds of pressure. His shop was busy during the war, producing parts and teaching younger machinists the trade.

The first elementary school I attended was James Whitcomb Riley School on Charles Page Boulevard in Tulsa. I rode the streetcar from Tulsa to Sand Springs to get there. The school was eventually torn down, but I returned after the war to visit my old teachers who remembered me, especially because I struggled with spelling until my dad helped me improve.

Charles Page Boulevard was the location of my elementary school, James Whitcomb Riley School, in Tulsa. It was along the streetcar line that ran between Tulsa and Sand Springs, which I took to school every day. This area was part of my early childhood and schooling experience.

Sand Springs, Oklahoma, was connected to Tulsa by a streetcar line that I rode to get to James Whitcomb Riley School on Charles Page Boulevard. The streetcar came every 20 minutes and was an important part of my daily commute as a student. It was a familiar route during my childhood.

After elementary school, I went to Roosevelt Junior High School, which is near where I live now in Tulsa. The school building is still there but now serves as a special education school. It was part of my education before I moved on to Central High School.

I attended Central High School at 6th and Cincinnati in Tulsa and graduated in 1941. I was a senior when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941. Later, my own children went to the new Central High School built out on West Edison, continuing the family connection to the school.

I was a senior in high school on December 7, 1941, when the news broke that the Japanese had bombed Pearl Harbor. At first, we didn’t know where Pearl Harbor was or what it meant, but it quickly became clear how serious the situation was. The attack changed everything, and many boys wanted to enlist immediately.

After graduating high school, I worked for Mid-Continent Petroleum Corporation in their laboratory testing oil. This was before it became known as Sun Oil Company. I enjoyed the work because I had been my chemistry teacher’s assistant in high school and loved figuring out how things worked.

Mid-Continent Petroleum Corporation, where I first worked after high school, later became Sun Oil Company. I spent about 10 months working in their lab before I was drafted into the Marine Corps during World War II. The company had many men leave for the war, and women took over much of the lab work while I was there.

In 1943, I was drafted into the United States Marine Corps and sent to boot camp in San Diego, California. I chose the Marines because I liked their blue uniform. Boot camp was tough; we were downgraded psychologically and physically to build us up as Marines. They taught us close-order drill, weapons training, and how to fight with knives, bayonets, and sticks.

After being drafted, I traveled from Oklahoma City to San Diego, California, for Marine Corps boot camp. The train ride was uncomfortable and crowded, with no seats and a bad smell from babies and dirty diapers. San Diego was where I underwent the tough training that transformed me into a Marine.

I had to catch a bus from Tulsa to Oklahoma City to board a train to San Diego for Marine Corps boot camp. The information I was given about the train accommodations was wrong, and I ended up standing in a crowded coach car for two days and a night. Oklahoma City was a key transit point for me going into the service.

During Marine boot camp, we trained extensively with the M1 Garand rifle, which weighed about eight pounds. We spent three weeks practicing on gravel, learning to sight in using a tomato can lid with a hole punched in it before firing live rounds. The M1 was the main weapon we learned to use for combat.

Before we trained with the M1 Garand, we used the M1903 Springfield rifle, which was an older model from World War I. It was part of our initial weapons training during boot camp. The transition to the M1 Garand was a big step in our preparation for combat.
